stem: Several factors affect GH secretion. What is the final common pathway through which most stimuli exert their effects on GH secretion?
options: Through an increase in GHRH levels and a decrease in somatostatin (GHIH) levels
Through a decrease in GHRH levels and an increase in somatostatin (GHIH) levels
Through a simultaneous increase in both GHRH levels and somatostatin (GHIH) levels
Through direct stimulation of somatotropes by peripheral hormones without involving GHRH or somatostatin

stem: GHRH and GHIH have distinct molecular sizes. Which description is MOST precisely correct?
options: GHRH is a 44 amino acid polypeptide, while GHIH has a variant of 14 amino acids and 28 amino acid polypeptides
GHRH is a 28 amino acid polypeptide, while GHIH has a variant of 14 amino acids and 44 amino acid polypeptides
GHRH is a 44 amino acid polypeptide, while GHIH has a variant of 10 amino acids and 28 amino acid polypeptides
GHRH is a 44 amino acid polypeptide, while GHIH has a variant of 14 amino acids and 22 amino acid polypeptides

stem: CASE STUDY: A student asks where the cells that produce GHRH are located and how GHRH stimulates GH release. Which is MOST precisely correct?
options: GHRH-producing cells are in the ventromedial nucleus of the hypothalamus; GHRH attaches to receptors on somatotrope cell membranes leading to an increase in CAMP, Ca2+, and phosphatidyl inositol; this stimulates GH release from secretory vesicles by exocytosis immediately and later stimulates transcription and synthesis of new GH

GHRH-producing cells are in the paraventricular nucleus of the hypothalamus; GHRH attaches to receptors on somatotrope cell membranes leading to activation of G-proteins and generation of IP3 and DAG; this stimulates GH release from secretory vesicles

GHRH-producing cells are in the arcuate nucleus of the hypothalamus; GHRH attaches to receptors on somatotrope cell membranes leading to JAK-STAT activation; this stimulates GH gene transcription but not immediate release

GHRH-producing cells are in the ventromedial nucleus of the hypothalamus; GHRH attaches to receptors on somatotrope cell membranes leading to a decrease in CAMP; this stimulates exocytosis of GH from secretory vesicles

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q3): GH release occurs in a specific pattern. What triggers this pattern?
options: GH release occurs in pulses triggered by the intermittent discharge of GHRH
GH release occurs in a continuous tonic pattern determined by the basal level of GHRH
GH release occurs in a circadian pattern triggered by a single daily surge of GHRH at night
GH release occurs in pulses triggered by the pulsatile secretion of somatostatin

stem: Ghrelin is described as a GH secretagogue. Which characteristics are MOST precisely stated?
options: Ghrelin is a 28-amino acid GH secretagogue peptide whose leading production site is the stomach; it is also produced in the hypothalamus and has GH-stimulating activity
Ghrelin is a 44-amino acid GH secretagogue peptide whose leading production site is the hypothalamus; it is also produced in the stomach and has GH-stimulating activity
Ghrelin is a 28-amino acid GH secretagogue peptide whose leading production site is the pancreas; it is also produced in the stomach and has GH-stimulating activity
Ghrelin is a 14-amino acid GH secretagogue peptide whose leading production site is the stomach; it is also produced in the intestine and has GH-stimulating activity

stem: GHIH inhibits GH production by affecting specific second messengers. Which is MOST precisely stated?
options: GHIH inhibits GH production by reducing CAMP and phosphatidylinositol's second messenger phosphatidyl inositol
GHIH inhibits GH production by reducing CAMP and increasing somatostatin levels further through positive feedback
GHIH inhibits GH production by increasing CAMP and reducing phosphatidyl inositol levels
GHIH inhibits GH production by blocking GHRH receptors on somatotrope cell membranes without affecting second messengers

stem: CASE STUDY: A nurse lists physiological factors that STIMULATE GH secretion. Which combination is MOST precisely complete?
options: Nutritional factors (reduced blood sugar, reduced FFA, starvation, fasting, infusion of amino acids especially arginine); stress-induced factors (trauma, emotions, excitement, exercise); sleep â deep sleep Stages II and IV exhibiting circadian rhythm
Nutritional factors (elevated blood sugar, elevated FFA, starvation, fasting); stress-induced factors (trauma, emotions, excitement, exercise); sleep â REM sleep exhibiting circadian rhythm

Nutritional factors (reduced blood sugar, reduced FFA, starvation, fasting, infusion of amino acids especially leucine); stress-induced factors (trauma, emotions, excitement, exercise); sleep â deep sleep Stages III and IV

Nutritional factors (reduced blood sugar, reduced FFA, starvation, fasting, infusion of amino acids especially arginine); stress-induced factors (trauma, emotions, excitement, infection); sleep â deep sleep Stages II and IV

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q7): The nurse then lists physiological factors that DECREASE GH secretion. Which combination is MOST precisely stated?
options: Postprandial elevation of blood sugar; elevated free fatty acids; ageing; rapid eye movement (REM, light sleep) sleep
Postprandial elevation of blood sugar; reduced free fatty acids; ageing; deep sleep stages II and IV
Postprandial elevation of blood sugar; elevated free fatty acids; puberty; rapid eye movement sleep
Fasting; elevated free fatty acids; ageing; rapid eye movement sleep

stem: Pharmacological factors that STIMULATE GH secretion are listed. Which hormonal combination is MOST precisely correct?
options: GHRH, ACTH, glucagon, testosterone, and oestrogen
GHRH, TSH, glucagon, testosterone, and progesterone
GHRH, ACTH, insulin, testosterone, and oestrogen
GHRH, ACTH, glucagon, cortisol, and oestrogen

stem: CASE STUDY: A student is asked to identify the adrenergic drug class and the specific drug that STIMULATES GH secretion. Which is MOST precise?
options: Alpha adrenergic agonist (clonidine) stimulates GH secretion; beta-adrenergic antagonist (propranolol) stimulates GH secretion
Alpha adrenergic antagonist (phentolamine) stimulates GH secretion; beta-adrenergic agonist (isopreterenol) stimulates GH secretion
Beta adrenergic agonist (clonidine) stimulates GH secretion; alpha-adrenergic antagonist (propranolol) stimulates GH secretion
Alpha adrenergic agonist (epinephrine) stimulates GH secretion; beta-adrenergic antagonist (atenolol) stimulates GH secretion

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q10): The student identifies which drugs INHIBIT GH secretion. Which combination is MOST precisely correct?
options: Hormones: somatostatin, GH, progesterone, glucocorticoids; alpha adrenergic antagonist (phentolamine); beta-adrenergic agonist (isopreterenol); serotonin agonist; dopamine antagonist
Hormones: somatostatin, GH, oestrogen, glucocorticoids; alpha adrenergic antagonist (phentolamine); beta-adrenergic agonist (isopreterenol); serotonin agonist; dopamine antagonist
Hormones: somatostatin, GH, progesterone, mineralocorticoids; alpha adrenergic antagonist (phentolamine); beta-adrenergic agonist (isopreterenol); serotonin agonist; dopamine antagonist
Hormones: somatostatin, GH, testosterone, glucocorticoids; alpha adrenergic antagonist (phentolamine); beta-adrenergic agonist (isopreterenol); serotonin agonist; dopamine antagonist

stem: Pathological factors that STIMULATE GH secretion include which combination?
options: Protein-energy malnutrition; anorexia nervosa; chronic renal failure
Protein-energy malnutrition; anorexia nervosa; acromegaly
Protein-energy malnutrition; obesity; chronic renal failure
Protein-energy malnutrition; anorexia nervosa; thyroid dysfunction

stem: CASE STUDY: A nurse asks about pathological factors that DECREASE GH secretion. Which is MOST precisely correct?
options: Obesity; thyroid dysfunction; acromegaly
Obesity; anorexia nervosa; acromegaly
Obesity; thyroid dysfunction; Laron syndrome
Protein-energy malnutrition; thyroid dysfunction; acromegaly

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q13): The nurse explains the GH feedback regulation loop. Which is MOST precisely described?
options: GH increases circulating IGF-1; IGF-1 acts on the pituitary through a long-loop mechanism to inhibit GH secretion; simultaneously it acts on the hypothalamus via a more extended loop feedback mechanism to stimulate the production of somatostatin

GH increases circulating IGF-1; IGF-1 acts on the hypothalamus through a long-loop mechanism to stimulate GH secretion; simultaneously it acts on the pituitary via a short-loop mechanism to inhibit GHRH release
GH increases circulating somatostatin; somatostatin acts on the pituitary through a long-loop mechanism to inhibit GH secretion; simultaneously GH acts on the hypothalamus to stimulate GHRH release
GH directly inhibits its own secretion at the somatotrope level through an ultra-short loop mechanism without involving IGF-1 or hypothalamic feedback

stem: Causes of pituitary insufficiency are listed. Which set is MOST completely and correctly derived from the notes?
options: Empty sella syndrome; Levi-Lorain syndromes; hypothalamic failure affecting GHRH; congenital tumours and cysts involving the Sella tursica; GH receptor insensitivity; tuberculous meningitis; encephalitis; chromophobe adenoma; Sheehan's syndrome; irradiation

Empty sella syndrome; Levi-Lorain syndromes; hypothalamic failure affecting GHRH; congenital tumours and cysts involving the Sella tursica; GH receptor insensitivity; bacterial meningitis; encephalitis; chromophobe adenoma; Sheehan's syndrome; irradiation

Empty sella syndrome; Levi-Lorain syndromes; hypothalamic failure affecting CRH; congenital tumours and cysts involving the Sella tursica; GH receptor insensitivity; tuberculous meningitis; encephalitis; chromophobe adenoma; Sheehan's syndrome; irradiation

Empty sella syndrome; Levi-Lorain syndromes; hypothalamic failure affecting GHRH; congenital tumours and cysts involving the Sella tursica; GH receptor insensitivity; tuberculous meningitis; meningococcal meningitis; chromophobe adenoma; Sheehan's syndrome; irradiation

stem: CASE STUDY: A post-partum woman develops panhypopituitarism after massive haemorrhage at delivery. Which cause of pituitary insufficiency is MOST likely?
options: Sheehan's syndrome â pituitary necrosis due to postpartum haemorrhage causing ischaemia of the pituitary
Empty sella syndrome â subarachnoid space invades the sella after delivery due to hormonal changes
Chromophobe adenoma â a non-secreting pituitary tumour that enlarges during pregnancy due to oestrogen stimulation
Levi-Lorain syndrome â a congenital cause of pituitary insufficiency triggered by the stress of childbirth

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q16): A child with GH receptor insensitivity (Laron syndrome) is identified. Which clinical consequence is MOST directly predicted?
options: GH cannot act on its target tissues despite normal or elevated GH levels; somatomedins (IGF-1) are not produced; the result is short stature despite normal or high GH levels
GH cannot be produced by somatotropes; IGF-1 levels are low because the liver lacks GH stimulation; the result is short stature with low GH levels
GHRH cannot stimulate somatotropes; GH levels are very low; IGF-1 levels are very low; the result is growth failure with absent pulsatile GH secretion
GH receptor insensitivity leads to excessive GH accumulation causing paradoxical overstimulation of IGF-1 through alternative pathways

stem: The physiology of growth explains that in intrauterine life, the hormonal factor responsible for foetal growth is believed to be a specific substance. Which is MOST precisely stated?
options: The hormonal factor responsible for foetal growth is believed to be foetal IGF-2, which is stimulated by human placental lactogen from the placenta
The hormonal factor responsible for foetal growth is believed to be foetal IGF-1, which is stimulated by maternal GH crossing the placenta
The hormonal factor responsible for foetal growth is believed to be foetal GH, which is secreted by the fetal pituitary from early gestation
The hormonal factor responsible for foetal growth is believed to be foetal IGF-2, which is stimulated by foetal insulin from the pancreas

stem: CASE STUDY: A student asks about the pattern of postnatal growth. Which statement is MOST precisely stated?
options: During the first year of postnatal life the rate of growth is rapid though less than in utero and is called the primary growth spurt; growth continues at a reduced rate until late puberty when a secondary growth spurt occurs
During the first year of postnatal life the rate of growth is rapid and greater than in utero and is called the primary growth spurt; growth continues at a reduced rate until early puberty

During the first year of postnatal life the rate of growth is slower than in utero and is called the secondary growth spurt; growth continues at an increased rate until late puberty when a tertiary growth spurt occurs
During the first year of postnatal life the rate of growth is rapid equal to that in utero and is called the primary growth spurt; growth continues at an increased rate throughout childhood

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q19): The secondary growth spurt at puberty is stimulated by a complex interaction of hormones. Which combination is MOST precisely listed?
options: Androgens with a profound protein anabolic effect; sex steroids bring about protein anabolism, increase GH response to arginine, and increase IGF-1 production; oestrogens have growth-stimulating ability but with a tendency to induce early epiphyseal closure; insulin enhances growth-stimulating ability

Oestrogens with a profound protein anabolic effect; sex steroids bring about protein catabolism; androgens increase GH response to arginine and increase IGF-1 production; insulin inhibits growth-stimulating ability

Androgens with a profound protein anabolic effect; sex steroids bring about protein anabolism, increase GH response to arginine, and increase IGF-2 production; oestrogens induce early epiphyseal closure; insulin enhances growth-stimulating ability

Androgens with a profound protein anabolic effect; sex steroids bring about protein anabolism and decrease GH response to arginine; oestrogens increase IGF-1 production; insulin enhances growth-stimulating ability

stem: Thyroid hormones have a specific role in growth. Which is MOST precisely stated?
options: Thyroid hormones have permissive action on GH; appear to be necessary for the normal rate of GH secretion; and improve cartilage ossification and growth of teeth
Thyroid hormones directly stimulate GH secretion; are the most important hormones for the primary growth spurt; and promote epiphyseal closure at puberty
Thyroid hormones antagonise GH action; suppress IGF-1 production; and have no effect on cartilage ossification
Thyroid hormones have an inhibitory effect on GH secretion; appear to be necessary for the control of the secondary growth spurt only

stem: CASE STUDY: A student asks about the effect of adrenal cortical hormones on growth. Which is MOST precisely stated?
options: Adrenal cortical hormones other than androgens exert a permissive action on growth; but glucocorticoids inhibit growth; after the secondary growth spurt, cessation of growth occurs and this has been linked to oestrogen-induced epiphyseal closure
All adrenal cortical hormones including glucocorticoids exert a permissive action on growth; none of them inhibit growth
Adrenal cortical androgens are the only adrenal hormones that affect growth; glucocorticoids have no effect on growth velocity
Adrenal cortical hormones other than androgens directly stimulate GH secretion; glucocorticoids inhibit growth only when administered pharmacologically

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q22): The secondary growth spurt appears at different times in girls compared to boys. What is the clinical consequence?
options: The secondary growth spurt appears earlier in girls than boys; the consequence is early closure of epiphysis making girls shorter than their male counterparts of the same age, genetic, and socioeconomic background
The secondary growth spurt appears later in girls than boys; the consequence is delayed epiphyseal closure making girls taller than their male counterparts
The secondary growth spurt appears at the same time in girls and boys; the difference in adult height is due solely to differences in oestrogen levels
The secondary growth spurt appears earlier in girls than boys; the consequence is prolonged epiphyseal growth making girls taller than their male counterparts transiently

stem: Children exhibit a unique growth catch-up phenomenon. Which statement is MOST precisely stated?
options: Children exhibit a period of growth to catch up after illness or starvation; the growth rate during this period is greater than normal; the mechanism is unknown; nutritional factors may play a prominent role
Children exhibit a period of growth to catch up after illness or starvation; the growth rate during this period is equal to normal; the mechanism involves GH hypersecretion
Children exhibit a period of growth to catch up after illness only; starvation does not trigger catch-up growth because nutrient deficiency prevents IGF-1 production
Children exhibit a period of growth to catch up after illness or starvation; the growth rate during this period is slightly below normal; the mechanism involves thyroid hormone upregulation

stem: Local growth factors are polypeptides that stimulate growth of various cells. They can be divided into three groups. Which combination of all three groups is MOST precisely correct?
options: Agents that promote multiplication and development of various cell types (IGF, EGF, FGF, NGF, PDGF, angiogenic growth factor); cytokines (hormone-like molecules from lymphocytes, macrophages, and other cell types acting in paracrine manner); colony stimulating factors (regulate proliferation and maturation of red and white blood cells)

Agents that promote multiplication and development (IGF, EGF, FGF, NGF, PDGF, angiogenic growth factor); cytokines (from lymphocytes and macrophages acting in endocrine manner); colony stimulating factors (regulate only white blood cell proliferation)

Agents that promote multiplication (IGF, EGF, FGF, NGF, PDGF); cytokines (from lymphocytes and macrophages acting in paracrine manner); colony stimulating factors (regulate only red blood cell maturation)

Agents that promote multiplication of cell types (IGF, EGF, FGF, NGF, PDGF, angiogenic growth factor); autocrine factors; colony stimulating factors (regulate proliferation and maturation of red and white blood cells)

stem: CASE STUDY: A student asks how agents that promote cell multiplication primarily signal. Which is MOST precisely stated?
options: Most of these agents act via enzyme-linked cell membrane receptors (receptor tyrosine kinases, RTKs) which are activated via autophosphorylation; insulin and macrophage colony-stimulating factors act through this mechanism
Most of these agents act via G-protein coupled receptors which generate cAMP as the primary second messenger; insulin acts through this mechanism
Most of these agents act via ion-channel receptors that change membrane permeability; insulin and IGF act through this mechanism
Most of these agents act via nuclear receptors that directly activate gene transcription; growth factors diffuse across membranes to bind intracellular receptors

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q26): Cytokines are described as a specific category of growth-regulating molecules. Which description is MOST precisely correct?
options: Cytokines are hormone-like molecules produced by lymphocytes, macrophages, and other cell types; they act in a paracrine manner; examples include interleukins and interferons; cell signalling is via the stimulation of transmembrane cytokine receptors leading to the stimulation of JAK-STAT pathways

Cytokines are hormone-like molecules produced exclusively by lymphocytes; they act in an endocrine manner; examples include interleukins and growth hormone; cell signalling is via G-protein coupled receptors
Cytokines are protein molecules produced by the liver; they act in an endocrine manner; examples include interleukins and complement proteins; cell signalling is via nuclear receptors

Cytokines are hormone-like molecules produced by lymphocytes and pituitary cells; they act in an autocrine manner exclusively; examples include interferons and ACTH; cell signalling is via RTK autophosphorylation

stem: Which hormones act via the JAK-STAT pathway according to the cytokine pathway notes?
options: Prolactin, erythropoietin, thrombopoietin, and granulocyte colony-stimulating factors act via this mechanism
Prolactin, erythropoietin, insulin, and granulocyte colony-stimulating factors act via this mechanism
Prolactin, ACTH, thrombopoietin, and granulocyte colony-stimulating factors act via this mechanism
Prolactin, erythropoietin, thrombopoietin, and IGF-1 act via this mechanism

stem: Colony stimulating factors (CSFs) regulate specific cells. Which is MOST precisely stated?
options: Colony stimulating factors regulate the proliferation and maturation of red and white blood cells; an example is granulocyte monocytes colony-stimulating factor (GM-CSF); signal transduction is via RTK and/or cytokine receptors

Colony stimulating factors regulate only white blood cell proliferation; an example is granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF); signal transduction is via G-protein coupled receptors exclusively
Colony stimulating factors regulate the proliferation and maturation of platelets only; an example is thrombopoietin; signal transduction is via JAK-STAT pathways
Colony stimulating factors regulate the differentiation of stem cells into all blood cell types; an example is erythropoietin; signal transduction is via nuclear receptors

stem: CASE STUDY: A student is asked what the 'most important extrinsic factor' for optimal growth is. Which is MOST precisely stated?
options: Nutrition is the most important extrinsic factor; adequate protein, calories, and micronutrients are needed if optimal growth must be achieved
Hormones are the most important extrinsic factor; GH, thyroid hormones, and sex steroids are required if optimal growth must be achieved
Genetic factors are the most important extrinsic factor; parental height determines the growth potential that must be supported for optimal achievement
Sleep is the most important extrinsic factor; deep sleep stages II and IV are required because GH is secreted primarily during this time

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q30): The student asks about agents that promote cell multiplication. Which specific growth factors are MOST completely listed?
options: IGF, epidermal growth factor, fibroblast growth factor, nerve growth factor, platelet-derived growth factor, and angiogenic growth factor
IGF, epidermal growth factor, fibroblast growth factor, nerve growth factor, platelet-derived growth factor, and transforming growth factor
IGF, epidermal growth factor, fibroblast growth factor, nerve growth factor, vascular endothelial growth factor, and angiogenic growth factor
IGF, epidermal growth factor, fibroblast growth factor, nerve growth factor, platelet-derived growth factor, and colony-stimulating growth factor

stem: Dopamine agonist is listed as a pharmacological stimulator of GH secretion. Which drug is implied by this classification?
options: Dopamine agonist stimulates GH secretion; this is a pharmacological factor listed alongside clonidine and propranolol
Dopamine antagonist stimulates GH secretion; this is listed as a pharmacological factor that promotes GH release
Serotonin agonist stimulates GH secretion; dopamine agonist inhibits GH secretion by promoting somatostatin release
Dopamine agonist inhibits GH secretion; this is why dopamine antagonists are used to stimulate GH release in testing

stem: CASE STUDY: A pharmacology student asks which serotonergic drug promotes GH secretion. Based on the notes, the answer is:
options: Serotonin precursors stimulate GH secretion
Serotonin agonists inhibit GH secretion
Serotonin antagonists stimulate GH secretion
Serotonin reuptake inhibitors stimulate GH secretion via increased synaptic serotonin

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q33): The pharmacology student then asks which beta-adrenergic drug INHIBITS GH secretion. Which is MOST precisely stated?
options: Beta-adrenergic agonist (isopreterenol) inhibits GH secretion
Beta-adrenergic antagonist (propranolol) inhibits GH secretion
Beta-adrenergic agonist (salbutamol) inhibits GH secretion
Beta-adrenergic antagonist (atenolol) inhibits GH secretion

stem: The most important control of GH secretion is probably mediated through which hypothalamic hormone?
options: Most of the control of GH secretion is probably mediated through GHRH
Most of the control of GH secretion is probably mediated through somatostatin (GHIH)
Most of the control of GH secretion is probably mediated through ghrelin
Most of the control of GH secretion is probably mediated through dopamine

stem: CASE STUDY: A student asks what second messengers are generated when GHRH binds to somatotrope cell membranes. Which combination is MOST precisely stated?
options: GHRH leads to an increase in CAMP, Ca2+, and phosphatidyl inositol
GHRH leads to an increase in CAMP and Ca2+ only; phosphatidyl inositol is not involved
GHRH leads to a decrease in CAMP and an increase in Ca2+ and phosphatidyl inositol
GHRH leads to an increase in IP3 and DAG via phospholipase-C without CAMP involvement

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q36): Immediately after GHRH stimulation of somatotropes, what is the IMMEDIATE response versus the LATER response?
options: Immediate response: GH release from secretory vesicles by exocytosis; later response: stimulates transcription and synthesis of new GH
Immediate response: transcription and synthesis of new GH; later response: GH release from secretory vesicles by exocytosis
Immediate response: activation of JAK-STAT pathway; later response: GH release from secretory vesicles without new synthesis
Immediate response and later response are identical; GHRH stimulates continuous exocytosis without any transcriptional component

stem: Which specific sleep stages stimulate GH secretion and what rhythm does this exhibit?
options: Deep sleep Stages II and IV stimulate GH secretion and exhibit circadian rhythm
REM sleep (light sleep) stimulates GH secretion and exhibits ultradian rhythm
Deep sleep Stages III and IV stimulate GH secretion and exhibit circadian rhythm
Deep sleep Stages I and II stimulate GH secretion and exhibit circadian rhythm

stem: CASE STUDY: A nurse is asked about the nutritional amino acid that specifically stimulates GH secretion. Which is MOST precisely stated?
options: Infusion of amino acids especially arginine stimulates GH secretion
Infusion of amino acids especially leucine stimulates GH secretion
Infusion of amino acids especially glutamine stimulates GH secretion
Infusion of amino acids especially tryptophan stimulates GH secretion

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q39): The nurse explains that during fasting, GH secretion changes. Based on the notes, which change occurs?
options: Fasting stimulates GH secretion because it represents a nutritional factor with reduced blood sugar and reduced free fatty acids
Fasting inhibits GH secretion because low nutrient levels reduce the substrate available for new GH synthesis
Fasting has no effect on GH secretion because GH is regulated exclusively by hypothalamic GHRH and somatostatin
Fasting initially stimulates then inhibits GH secretion through a biphasic nutritional feedback mechanism

stem: The Sella turcica is mentioned in the context of pituitary insufficiency. Which conditions specifically involve the Sella turcica?
options: Congenital tumours and cysts involving the Sella tursica are a cause of pituitary insufficiency
Chromophobe adenoma involving the Sella tursica is the only pituitary mass causing insufficiency
Empty sella syndrome involves the Sella tursica being completely destroyed
Sheehan's syndrome involves haemorrhage specifically into the Sella tursica

stem: CASE STUDY: An examiner asks which specific amino acid that is the most important for GH secretion testing. Based on the notes this is:
options: Arginine is the specifically mentioned amino acid whose infusion stimulates GH secretion
Leucine is the specifically mentioned amino acid whose infusion stimulates GH secretion
Tryptophan is the specifically mentioned amino acid whose infusion stimulates GH secretion
Glutamine is the specifically mentioned amino acid whose infusion stimulates GH secretion

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q42): A student asks about alpha adrenergic antagonist drugs that INHIBIT GH secretion. Which drug is specifically named?
options: Phentolamine is the alpha adrenergic antagonist that inhibits GH secretion
Prazosin is the alpha adrenergic antagonist that inhibits GH secretion
Phenoxybenzamine is the alpha adrenergic antagonist that inhibits GH secretion
Terazosin is the alpha adrenergic antagonist that inhibits GH secretion

stem: GHRH attaches to somatotrope receptors and the cell signalling leads to GH release in a specific way. What is the specific release mechanism?
options: GH is released from secretory vesicles by exocytosis
GH is released from secretory vesicles by endocytosis
GH is released from the endoplasmic reticulum by active transport
GH is released from the Golgi apparatus by constitutive secretion

stem: CASE STUDY: A student is asked to identify the complete list of stress-induced factors that stimulate GH secretion. Which is MOST precisely stated?
options: Trauma, emotions, excitement, exercise
Trauma, emotions, excitement, infection
Trauma, fear, excitement, exercise
Trauma, emotions, anxiety, exercise

stem: CASE STUDY (continued from Q45): The secondary growth spurt is stimulated by the complex interaction of hormones. After this spurt, what causes growth to cease?
options: Cessation of growth after the secondary growth spurt has been linked to oestrogen-induced epiphyseal closure
Cessation of growth after the secondary growth spurt has been linked to androgen-induced epiphyseal closure
Cessation of growth after the secondary growth spurt has been linked to GH-induced epiphyseal closure
Cessation of growth after the secondary growth spurt has been linked to thyroid hormone-induced epiphyseal closure

stem: The process of growth is multifarious. What three broad categories of factors influence it?
options: Hormones, genetic factors, and nutrition
Hormones, environmental factors, and nutrition
Genetic factors, environmental factors, and nutrition
Hormones, genetic factors, and sleep

stem: The final common pathway through which most factors affect GH secretion is through an increase in GHRH levels and a decrease in _______________ levels.
CASE STUDY: An examiner asks: 'GHRH is a _______________ amino acid polypeptide.' One number must be supplied.
GHIH has a variant of _______________ amino acids and 28 amino acid polypeptides.
CASE STUDY: An examiner asks: 'GHRH-producing cells are found in the _______________ nucleus of the hypothalamus.' One precise anatomical term must be supplied.
Ghrelin is a _______________ amino acid GH secretagogue peptide whose leading production site is the stomach.
CASE STUDY: An exam states: 'GHIH inhibits GH production by reducing _______________ and phosphatidylinositol second messengers.' One second messenger must be supplied.
Among physiological factors that decrease GH secretion, sleep type specifically listed is _______________ eye movement (light sleep) sleep.
CASE STUDY: An examiner asks: 'Among pharmacological stimulators of GH secretion, the alpha adrenergic agonist listed is _______________.' One drug name must be supplied.
Among pharmacological inhibitors of GH secretion, the alpha adrenergic antagonist listed is _______________ and the beta-adrenergic agonist is isopreterenol.

CASE STUDY: An exam asks: 'Among pathological factors that stimulate GH secretion, chronic _______________ failure is listed alongside protein-energy malnutrition and anorexia nervosa.' One organ must be supplied.
The foetal hormone responsible for foetal growth is believed to be foetal _______________ which is stimulated by human placental lactogen from the placenta.

CASE STUDY: An examiner states: 'The rate of growth during the first year of postnatal life is rapid though less than _______________ and is called the primary growth spurt.' Two words must be supplied.
Oestrogens have the growth-stimulating ability but with a tendency to induce early _______________ closure.
CASE STUDY: An exam asks: 'Thyroid hormones have _______________ action on GH â they appear to be necessary for the normal rate of GH secretion.' One precise term must be supplied.
Adrenal cortical hormones other than androgens exert a permissive action on growth; but _______________ inhibit growth.

CASE STUDY: An exam states: 'The secondary growth spurt appears earlier in girls than boys; the consequence is early closure of epiphysis, a situation that makes girls _______________ than their male counterparts of the same age.' One adjective must be supplied.
Children are peculiar in that they exhibit a period of growth to catch up after illness or starvation; during this period the growth rate is _______________ than normal.

CASE STUDY: An examiner asks: 'Most agents that promote cell multiplication act via enzyme-linked cell membrane receptors called receptor _______________ kinases (RTKs) which are activated via autophosphorylation.' One word must be supplied.
Cytokines are hormone-like molecules produced by lymphocytes, macrophages, and other cell types; they act in a _______________ manner.
CASE STUDY: An exam asks: 'Colony stimulating factors regulate the proliferation and maturation of red and _______________ blood cells.' One cell type must be supplied.
GH increases circulating IGF-1; IGF-1 acts on the pituitary through a _______________ loop mechanism to inhibit GH secretion.

CASE STUDY: An examiner states: 'Causes of pituitary insufficiency include _______________ syndrome, which involves pituitary necrosis caused by postpartum haemorrhage.' One eponymous name must be supplied.
Sex steroids bring about protein anabolism, increase GH response to _______________, and increase IGF-1 production during the secondary growth spurt.
